reasoning:
The total volume of Russian natural gas imported to the EU in 2024 was around 52 bcm (an average of 13 bcm per quarter). Throughout 2024, the primary routes were the Ukraine transit pipeline, TurkStream, and LNG shipments. In early 2025, the Ukraine transit arrangement officially ceased, effectively removing roughly 15 bcm/year (or ~3.75 bcm/quarter) of pipeline export capacity from Russia to the EU. 

Data for the first half of 2025 indicates that overall Russian gas exports to the EU experienced a sharp decline proportional to the lost transit route. In Q2 2025, volumes stood at roughly 9.5 bcm total for the quarter, and reports note this was an 8% increase compared to Q1 2025 (putting Q1 at roughly 8.8 bcm). Given that the EU's total import capacity from Russia is currently severely constrained by the lack of the Ukraine pipeline and capped TurkStream/LNG capacities, a jump from ~9.5 bcm to the required 15 bcm (a >55% increase) in either Q3 or Q4 of 2025 is effectively impossible barring a completely unexpected reopening of pipeline routes combined with a massive structural pivot back to Russian energy. The crowd forecast is 1%, aligning perfectly with the base-rate limit of 0.02.
